item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just a thing that turns animals into humans, and also it shows that MC&D clients are degenerates… It has some good body horror; but that note about the tapeworms was needlessly edgy in my opinion, it tries too hard to be scary. Not bad enough for a downvote, though.

item.title: JESUS CHRIST
item.content:encoded:
This was one of the first SCPs that I read, and I haven't encountered anything nearly as disturbing since. Though, I agree with the people above on that testing aquatic animals would be interesting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All I can think of is how I wanna see it tested with monkeys

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If by "less frequent" you mean "not drunk to completion," the end result is "partial transformation". If you mean it as "not drunk enough to trigger the transformation", the end result is "thirsty lab animal."

Also, funnily enough, this one posted up on my birthday and I never got the notice! I'mma have to make sure Verizon isn't sending all the email I actually WANT to get into the spam bin. Again.

item.title: Re: Additional Testing
item.content:encoded:
Short version: they would be able to learn English slightly faster. That's about it.

As far as penguins, I don't even know it wasn't brought up until testing was completed, determined superfluous and testing wasn't re-opened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For how fetishy the concept is, it's executed very well.
I'd love to see some cross-testing, but in lieu of the Foundation completely disregarding its principles, a snippet on the effects of less frequent use would be nice, as would the results of extremely large and/or aquatic animals.

item.title: Additional Testing
item.content:encoded:
I think that this SCP is neat, but I think some things should be added to the testing log:

  • More intelligent animals should be tested with this, such as dolphins, chimpanzees, and elephants. I would be curious as to what they say. Although the dolphin suggestion should probably not be done because for my first/newest SCP, demonstrating the true intelligence of dolphins is the central focus.
  • As Sonata Green said, you should test this with Penguins (from Antarctica, of course) to see what race/ethnicity they end up exhibiting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh God, screaming tapeworm people … *Whimpers*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this fits perfectly as a marshall carter and dark scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what sort of human racial features you'd get from a penguin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also: why did they have to use so many juvenile animals

I used 3 adults, 4 adolescent/preadolescent. That gives a variety of samplings; it's also fairly safe to say that it's easier to get younger specimens for scientific research. That's my understanding of it anyway.

As far as "curiously" goes, I'm not sure how better to put it, and doubt such a minor change at this point would change most peoples' thoughts on the entire matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this too. Also 'curiously' is poor tone.

But never mind, I like this enough.

Also: why did they have to use so many juvenile animals

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it for the creep-factor. I hadn't thought of the…weird furry-isms. Reading the comments made me like it less. Kind of neat skip, off putting. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but one nitpick:

Curiously, most modern breeds of dogs and cats will have indistinct or mixed racial features.

This seems anything but “curious.” What exactly would you call the native land of Canis lupus familiaris or Felis catus, besides, well, Earth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the tapeworm bit, reread it, read the discussion, went back, reread the tapeworm bit, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reverse furry maker, if you want to be precise; it's supposed to turn full animals into full humans, but it sadly has a rather high failure rate. Sadly for those involved, anyway.

And yes, MC&D are much more interested in the satisfaction of their customers than little things like the well-being of dumb animals. Or international law. Or physics and reason in general.

As far as the 0.01%? Well… I did say they *stabilized*. Not that the conversion was complete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is a furry maker if I ever saw one.

Aside from that, a typical MCD artifact that doesnt care for the suffering of those that it's used upon. Yuch.

I'm pretty curious what was the 0.01% non-mammals that survived.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 7 foot tape worm with screaming face bursting from intestines of dog…oh god…this is like…alien…except LONG…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, somehow never got the notice about this post and never saw it… I was specifically getting *away* from the furry theme of it. Especially since the original idea this was based on has become "The Furry Mirror" in the wiki's populous, and not in a positive way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lampshading the obvious furry theme might make it go over better with readers.

item.title: Dat tapeworm.
item.content:encoded: Just got my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, that's more or less what I was going for, I put it a bit to generally the first time and tried to make it more explicit this go-round.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't actually find the talking thing to be implausible, mainly because it's only a small amount of words, and not actual conversation.

My dogs have different barks for "Pay attention to me," "There's someone outside," "A family member is outside," "Give me people food," etc. To me, this means they have their own "words," even if they all sound really similar to us.

Moreover, you can train animals to do certain things. Not just to obey commands, but to tell us when they need something, such as standing by the door when they need to go outside.

It's not unreasonable that if we repeat the word "food" when we give them food, they learn to say "food" when they want it, especially since they now have the proper vocal cords and mouth to be able to say those words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a succession of incredibly famous people who lead incredibly diverse lives, yet all spoke in English with a horribly fake accent. And also their "real lives" didn't happen, it was all cover stories, the current pop-culture images were what ACTUALLY happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was Marie Antoinette's underwear, I promise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a fall webworm in my past life…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the Agent with dreams of becoming a Lemur was the main thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, Chazz. You see, guys, this is how you write an article with your head screwed on.

I don't know what the fuck I was thinking when I wrote the original. I wasn't going for a furry thing, but I should have know someone would misinterpret that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the line…. the psychologist is having a hard time helping Janet with her nightmares of a seven foot long tapeworm with a screaming face tearing out of the belly of a dog.

It's a good line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tapeworm is groovy. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1575 was recovered from a facility named "The Miracle Farm", previously owned and operated by Marshall, Carter & Dark Ltd. The stated goal of the facility was to "present our clients with the finest in custom-ordered pets, prey and companions."

…there is nothing about that second sentence that didn't make me cringe and want to drink a gallon of brain bleach. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely keeping the fleas and tapeworms, thanks for the tip on vocal cords (one of those things where the mistake is so common that people don't realize which one's the mistake)

I'm still working on the rewrite with the testing logs, trying to avoid overt redundancy and only point out some of the more spectacular failures. I'll take out the excessive expungement at that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it enough to upvote it. Some minor things:
1) "vocal chords" should be "vocal cords". You can use them to make chords, albeit only through some vocal trickery or more than one person, but they're cords of flesh that we use for vocalizing.
2) The expungement for the grizzly bear's excretions didn't ring true for me, especially since it was in the context of mass changes. My impression was that, in the losing of the weight, it contained excess biological materials and left me wondering why it would even be expunged.
3) Lastly, I say keep the fleas and tapeworms. It's noted that non-mammalian subjects are viable only in a small percentage of cases. On the other hand, most organisms of that type will number in the hundreds and thousands on a single host body… it's like that old joke that if you're "one in a million", there are 6,840 alive who are just like you. Small percentage meets large population.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first thought was something like "Groan, a ninja turtles generator" Followed quickly by an urge to hate it because, you know, furries. But then it touched on the realistic problems of converting animals to humanoids, and how it didn't really have a chance in hell of working on non-mamals, and I went neutral. Then I realized that it only makes girls, and I was back to hating a furries wish-fullfillment. Then the addenda clicked into Cronenberg horror, hard. I am left utterly conflicted about this. But the fact that it provokes such a roller-coaster of reactions at all makes me think that it must be good enough to keep around.

PS: I'll check it out when you get it up, but I can't think that a testing log would be anything but repetitive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, I'm taking some suggestions to heart here. I'm keeping it as MC&D and related materials, but I'm going to be adjusting the level of potential intelligence in the beings (something that I think didn't come across correctly when I first typed it in), as well as adding a testing log with a bit more description. This edit will probably be up later tonight or some time tomorrow while I work on it. Also, if anyone's good with coding tables on this wiki send me a buzz, and/or I can check with the IRC if I get the chance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's specifically why I said non-mammalian creatures don't *stabilize*, I never said that they're not AFFECTED. I'm also going to touch up the note to point out the fact that this was when they realized things could be affected by something ELSE that had drunk the water; it was a learning experience, just not in a good way. The fleas were in the middle of the "growth" phase, and a giant flea could still be the size of, like, a good sized wasp. The mutated tapeworm was nightmare fuel, pure and simple, and yes those things grow many feet in length if left untreated and they're able to continue to thrive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Part of me wants to go "IT MAKES FURRIES", but I feel that's mitigated somewhat by A) high mortality rates, and B) doesn't work on humans. I agree with whoever said they ought to keep their normal intelligence, rather than learning to speak (although animals which can produce sounds trying to do so with human vocal cords would be interesting).

Upvoted for the fucking tapeworm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone complaint, for me, is because the whole addendum comes across as "srsly guys. Srsly."

As for point 2, even if I did buy that explanation it still doesn't cover the catastrophic anatomical failure you'd get with the flea fairly rapidly. I don't know worms terribly well but I have to assume you'd get the same problem. The discrepancies in skeletal structure alone…. to say nothing of the circulatory system on the flear…. yeah….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
my main problem with it isnt about it breaking tone(though it does) or notes from researchers being overused or lame in general(which they are), but because it's poorly written. read it again. the first half isnt too bad, but then

We absolutely do not need another instance of giant half-human-half-flea creatures, and the psychologist is having a hard time helping Janet with her nightmares of a seven foot long tapeworm with a screaming face tearing out of the belly of a dog.

feels awkward and ridiculous to me in its phrasing, the "half-human-half-flea creatures" bit especially.

actually i think just stating the first half and then leaving it to the imagination of the reader would be more effective anyway(to give a slightly adjusted application of "The Fundamental Base of the Unsaid").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually kind of like this one. This is right up MC&D's alley, and rightfully squicky. I like the note at the end, it doesn't really seem lolfoundation, it is more like mentioning something that was legitimately overlooked and was traumatizing to the researchers. And I thought it was consistent with the previous remarks about non-mammals not surviving, I thought it implied that different parts of the animal would change at different rates, so lthe tapeworms human face could have started developing before other parts, but it was going to die before it became totally human.

I kind of agree with Smapti though, in that the manimals learning English seems a bit out there. On the other hand though, if they retained their animal instincts it would be kind of like other SCPs on the site, like the reconstructive maggots which I think conveyed the terrifying image of humans acting like blowflies really well (in a lives-in-my-nightmares kind of way). Also, wasn't there a horsefeed bag that did something like that too? Wait, I looked it up, it does the opposite, it makes horses act like a person. So, I dunno. Maybe it would be better if it was changed, or maybe it could just be removed entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Trust me, whoever let THAT particular SCP know about SCP-827 is going on immediate Keter duty. -O5-██"

Those older articles need some fixing up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fail to see how that one breaks the tone at all. Reminds me more of the Jaw Tree incident with SCP-827.
For one, the researchers weren't doing anything idiotic - what happened is an unexpected caveat.
For two.. consider the possibility that the reason the substance normally doesn't work well on non-mammals is that they lack something in their bodies that makes it into proper active form. The dog's parasites were taking it from the dog's blood, where whatever was needed already happened… and so the effect worked full steam on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, that's what it freaking says? Holy crap, I apologize. Jesus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the end note needs to go, and not just for tone. Given the caveats about non-mammals and creatures with small proportions, it simply doesn' make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except for that last addendum, I am really liking this. Especially the connection with MC&D. +1

EDIT: Scratch that last part about the addendum. When I read it again it was more terrifying than humorous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not bad. definitely better than the other article. this is EXACTLY the kind of thing i'd expect MC&D to have.

that said, lose the note at the end, it almost turned my upvote to a downvote all on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this should have a log for the expirements, give it more meat and horror than "it makes humans into animals and animals into humans".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um…
None of these addenda here really qualify as lolfoundation, besides, the image of a seven-foot tapeworm with a human screaming face tearing out of a dog in horror is one of the reasons I upvoted this rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, oddly enough. I'd recommend keeping the MC&D reference, it's something that really adds to the horror if you understand what they do. However, I also recommend removing the "lolfoundation" addenda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too fetishy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…
I actually like what you did here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A statue whose anomalous powers are induced by water? There's a skip for that!

On a serious note, though, i'm a little on the fence about this. I can buy the physiological transformation of an animal into a human being, but their acquiring human-level intelligence and magically learning to speak is a stretch for me. I think it'd be far more interesting if the transformees still had an animal mentality and were confused and horrified by their new bodies - I imagine that if you suddenly got turned into, say, a meerkat against your will, that you'd be none too happy about it.

Also, isn't the 16th century a little too late for Italian sculptors to be making erotic statues of Venus? I'm no art historian, but from what I can recall the Renaissance artists of the time were more into Biblical imagery than pagan gods.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is; was going less for "wish fulfillment" and more "inexplicable transmogrifier, repurposed to entertain obscenely rich folks".

Starting to think maybe I should remove the bit with MC&D, and instead make it having been used by a shady "biotech" firm…….. In fact I'm starting to think on that a LOT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking that, but the idea of working with test logs would basically be 3-Xty iterations of "It turned fully human" or "It turned most of the way human except/until this happened". Didn't think it enough to make its own blurb short of the conservation-of-mass exposition, may go with something like that if enough people think it'd be worth it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't really do anything for me. Tone's good, but I just don't like the idea at all. Also, the examples should be redone as Test Logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure this is a rewrite & attempted salvation of the idea behind Furry Mirror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of reminds me of the Furry Mirror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nghhhh It's well written, but the object itself is kind of…gross. And not in the good, creepy way. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the first part, that was me changing one half of the sentence and forgetting to change the other half. The streak continues, I always seem do that at least once per article.

As far as the distasteful and fetishy bit… Well, yes. That's half the point. It's a thing to use to change animals into people, or at least most of the way. We're not sure why it was made in the first place, whether intentions was particularly benign or sadistic, but we know that MC&D looked at it and said "Hey, you know, we know some folks that like pretty weird shit…"

And yes, in retrospect, the latter half of the note was a bit Lolfoundation, I excised it and tightened a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only █ non-mammalian mutations were known to have stabilized, and both were euthanized after brief study

So… 2 non-mammals, then.

Even with lampshading the furry thing as specialty products for MC&D, I still find this distasteful and fetishy.

Also, that humorous addendum really needs to go. If you want to include the parasites thing (which is actually a good idea), you need to do it in a non-humorous way. The funny note just blunts the potential horror, to the article's detriment.
